dialogue.utterance: Unit 3, respond to a diabetic emergency in the living room-59-year-old male reported high blood sugar and lethargy.
Hello, I'm Alex, and this is my partner Kevin. What's the main issue that led to your 911 call today?
My blood sugar has been showing 'HI' again, and I feel very tired.
He's been hard to rouse and hasn't eaten much today, similar to yesterday.
Jamie, can you tell me your name and where you are now?
Jamie Carter. I'm at home.
What day is today, and why did you call 911?
It's Wednesday. I didn't feel right with high blood sugar again.
Do you understand what's happening now? Are you aware of us?
Yes. I'm with you.
I'm checking your eyes now-can you follow the light for me, please?
Pupils are equal and reactive-PERRL-can you hear and follow me still?
Yes, yes. I hear you.
Can you wiggle your fingers and toes for me now, Jamie?
Okay.
Your airway is open and clear-no signs of obstruction. I'll check your breathing next.
That's fine with me.
Take a few deep breaths in for me.
Breath sounds are clear and equal bilaterally-no adventitious sounds-chest rise and fall are normal and symmetrical.
Your respiratory effort is normal with no signs of respiratory distress-chest movement symmetric-no signs of distress.
Strong, regular radial pulse-heart rate at 89. Skin is warm and dry with normal color and cap refill less than 2 seconds.
Jamie, have you had any specific symptoms other than the high blood glucose and tiredness? Like dizziness or chest discomfort?
No, just really thirsty and don't feel like eating much.
When was the last time you had food or drinks, Jamie?
He refused food all day. He didn't eat anything yesterday either, as far as I know.
When was the last time someone gave you insulin or any other medications, and how did you respond after that?
A nurse gave him insulin yesterday morning. But his condition got worse-still no improvement and blood sugar reading HI again today.
Jamie, do you have any allergies? You mentioned being allergic to something before-was it Lipitor?
Yes, I'm allergic to Lipitor. I told the nurse too.
I see. Any other medical concerns we should know about, like previous strokes or seizures?
Stroke-yes I had that. And I have seizures and diabetes and high blood pressure too.
He also has DVT and his stroke caused some speaking issues-expressive aphasia-some level of speech difficulty still present today.
Do you have any pain right now? Any location? Any pain when you move or breathe?
No-no pain in chest, abdomen, anywhere.
That's good. So you said no pain-any other discomfort or symptoms that you might be having now?
No-still just tired and thirsty.
What is the quality of your thirst or fatigue-can you describe it a bit more?
I just get really dry in the mouth and not motivated to move around.
On a scale of 0 to 10, with 10 being the worst, how would you rate your level of discomfort or symptoms you're experiencing now?
Maybe a 0.
Skin is normal in color, temperature, and moisture-no rashes, bruises, or signs of trauma noted-capillary refill less than 2 seconds.
Let me check your head and neck. Your pupils appear reactive and equal-can you follow the light with your eyes again?
No neck stiffness or tenderness. Gross visual exam of the head is unremarkable.
No nasal or ear discharge or bleeding. Throat examination reveals no obstruction, erythema, or swelling.
Jamie, we're now performing a 4 lead ECG. Is that okay?
Sure.
ECG recorded now as normal sinus rhythm with no significant ST abnormalities detected at this time.
Heart rate at 89-no change. Stay with us.
Respiratory rate is 16 per minute right now.
Blood pressure is 134 over 88.
Blood glucose is showing 'HI'-600 mg/dL-in the range of severe hyperglycemia-displaying at the maximum recognizing setting.
Oxygen saturation is at 100% on room air-no current respiratory compromise.
EKG remains in sinus rhythm-normal currently.
Glasgow Coma Scale shows you are alert and oriented to person, place, time-A&Ox3/4-with GCS of 14.
You appear normal, awake, and regular in your breathing with dry skin-NARD.
Positive peripheral motor system functioning in all four limbs-normal motor and sensory function-no deficits noted.
Blood glucose remains at 600-HI-that's consistent with hyperglycemia-no change from initial measurement.
Attempting IV access on the left arm for treatment of severe hyperglycemia. Any concerns with that, Jamie?
Okay.
First line IV access unsuccessful in the left arm-going to reposition for alternate attempts.
Now starting four lead EKG monitoring-continuously recording for any changes in rhythm.
EKG continues in sinus rhythm with no immediate changes-ECG started and documented.
Pulse still at 89-stable for the moment.
Respirations up to 18-this is an increase but still in the normal range.
Blood pressure has risen to 142 over 94-watching for fluctuation.
Blood sugar remains at HI-displaying beyond 600-confirming hyperglycemia ongoing.
Oxygen saturation now at 98%-a slight drop but still within normal range on room air.
EKG transitioned to sinus tachycardia-still non-threatening but progressing from initial reading.
Still experiencing no pain, only thirst and fatigue?
Yes, remains exactly the same.
No new symptoms reported since our initial assessment?
No new concerns at this time.
Dispatch-this is Unit 3-applying Protocol 3-7 for diabetic hyperglycemia-clinical picture stable and patient is fit for transport.
We'll be heading to [COMPANY_NAME] now for ED evaluation.
Now I'll assist you onto the stretcher.
Securely strapping you into the stretcher-ready to move you into the ambulance now.
Transporting to [COMPANY_NAME] as directed.
Loaded and monitoring as we head to the hospital.
ED has been alerted to the elevated glucose, and the patient's current presentation is stable.
Successfully transferred patient from EMS to hospital stretcher. Completed all standard handoffs and ongoing care documentation.
ED has been alerted of the hyperglycemia, current vitals, and history of CVA and expressive aphasia-no new issues during transport.
Patient transferred to the hospital's emergency department for further evaluation and diabetes management.
